coredata.dc:title: Recombinant Organophosphorus acid anhydrolase (OPAA) enzyme-carbon quantum dot (CQDs)-immobilized thin film biosensors for the specific detection of Ethyl Paraoxon and Methyl Parathion in water resources
coredata.dc:creator: Vyas, Tanmay
Jaiswal, Surbhi
Choudhary, Sandeep
Kodgire, Prashant
Joshi, Abhijeet
coredata.dc:description: Organophosphates pesticide (OP) toxicity through water resources is a large concern globally among all the emerging pollutants. Detection of OPs is a challenge which needs to be addressed considering the hazardous effects on the health of human beings. In the current research thin film biosensors of recombinant, Organophosphorus acid anhydrolase (OPAA) enzyme along with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) immobilized in thin films were developed. OPAA-CQDs thin film biosensors were used for the specific detection of two OPs Ethyl Paraoxon (EP) and Methyl Parathion (MP) in river water and household water supply. Recombinant OPAA enzyme was expressed in E. Coli, purified and immobilized on the CQD containing chitosan thin films. The CQDs used for this purpose were developed by a one-pot hydrothermal method from phthalic acid and Tri ethylene diamine. The properties of CQDs, OPAA and thin films were characterized using techniques like XPS, TEM, XRD, enzyme activity and CLSM measurements. Biosensing studies of EP and MP were performed by taking fluorescence measurements using a fiber optic spectrometer. The analytical parameters of biosensing were compared against an estimation carried out using the HPLC method. The biosensing performance indicates that the OPAA-CQDs thin film-based biosensors were able to detect both EP and MP in a range of 0–100 μM having a detection limit of 0.18 ppm/0.69 ppm for EP/MP, respectively with a response time of 5 min. The accuracy of estimation of EP/MP when spiked in water resources lie in the range of ∼100–102% which clearly indicates the OPAA-CQD based thin film biosensors can function as a point-of-use method for the detection of OP pesticides in complex water resources.

head.ce:title: Recombinant Organophosphorus acid anhydrolase (OPAA) enzyme-carbon quantum dot (CQDs)-immobilized thin film biosensors for the specific detection of Ethyl Paraoxon and Methyl Parathion in water resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organophosphates pesticide (OP) toxicity through water resources is a large concern globally among all the emerging pollutants. Detection of OPs is a challenge which needs to be addressed considering the hazardous effects on the health of human beings. In the current research thin film biosensors of recombinant, Organophosphorus acid anhydrolase (OPAA) enzyme along with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) immobilized in thin films were developed. OPAA-CQDs thin film biosensors were used for the specific detection of two OPs Ethyl Paraoxon (EP) and Methyl Parathion (MP) in river water and household water supply. Recombinant OPAA enzyme was expressed in E. Coli, purified and immobilized on the CQD containing chitosan thin films. The CQDs used for this purpose were developed by a one-pot hydrothermal method from phthalic acid and Tri ethylene diamine. The properties of CQDs, OPAA and thin films were characterized using techniques like XPS, TEM, XRD, enzyme activity and CLSM measurements. Biosensing studies of EP and MP were performed by taking fluorescence measurements using a fiber optic spectrometer. The analytical parameters of biosensing were compared against an estimation carried out using the HPLC method. The biosensing performance indicates that the OPAA-CQDs thin film-based biosensors were able to detect both EP and MP in a range of 0–100 μM having a detection limit of 0.18 ppm/0.69 ppm for EP/MP, respectively with a response time of 5 min. The accuracy of estimation of EP/MP when spiked in water resources lie in the range of ∼100–102% which clearly indicates the OPAA-CQD based thin film biosensors can function as a point-of-use method for the detection of OP pesticides in complex water resources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tanmay Vyas: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Validation. Surbhi Jaiswal: Data Curation, Visualization. Sandeep Choudhary: Software, Visualization, Data curation. Prashant Kodgire: Conceptualization, Supervision, Reviewing and Editing, Validation. Abhijeet Joshi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing- Original draft preparation Reviewing and Editing, Validation. All authors approve the final version of the manuscript.

Global population rise has led to increased requirements for food due to which the agriculture sector is pressed to use large quantities of pesticides. In many developing countries, around 1.8 billion people depend on agriculture for their livelihood (Pandey et al., 2016) and about 5.6 billion pounds of pesticides are used globally in the agriculture sector (Alavanja, 2009). Several classes of pesticides are common in use like Organophosphates, nitro compounds, amides, pyrethroids, organochlorine, and carbamates (Vyas et al., 2022). The pesticides typically are applied as combinations in mixed ratios for better influence over productivity. Among all the pesticides, organophosphate (OPs) based pesticides have been widely used due to their high efficacy and cost-effectiveness. Around 40% of the total pesticides are OP compounds globally (Kaushal et al., 2021).

Chemically OPs compounds have three phosphates diester bonds and any aryl (any halogen, aliphatic, aromatic) or alkyl group that is attached to phosphorous through an oxygen bond or sulfur bond (M. Jain et al., 2019). The use of pesticides has increased the food yields, however, have also started to show up their adverse effects on human health. Persistent exposure to OP pesticides leads to soil and water pollution occurring which causes accumulation of the OP pesticide residue to remain in the water, soil, crops, and various environmental sources. These residues of OP compounds in water resources cause several toxic health hazards to humans like carcinogenic effects, abdominal pain, nephrotoxic effects, etc. [6]. The main mechanism of OP pesticide action is the inactivation of Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) enzyme which is an enzyme degrading Acetyl Choline neurotransmitter. The prevention of degradation causes accumulation of Acetylcholine at the nerve junction causing excessive nerve stimulation leading to cholinergic effects and even paralysis (S. Wu et al., 2017). Due to their cholinergic toxic effect some of the OP compounds are also classified as nerve agents. The hazardous effects of OPs on humans make them a very important agent which needs to be detected in food, water, and the environment.

OPs have been classically detected using techniques like Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopic (SERS) (Pang et al., 2016), gas chromatography (GC) (van der Hoff and van Zoonen, 1999), High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Harshit et al., 2017), tandem mass spectrophotometry (Granby et al., 2004) etc. All these classical techniques offer high sensitivity and specificity towards OP pesticides but have various disadvantages like requirement of complex sample pretreatment, high cost, non-portability, complicated data analysis, time-consuming (Du et al., 2012). To overcome all these disadvantages there is an urgent requirement for the development of novel methods having properties of simple readout, portability, point-of-care nature, and highly sensitive detection of the OP pesticides.

Fluorescence based biosensing techniques are emerging tools that can provide fulfilment of all these requirements like specificity, sensitivity, a lower limit of detection (LOD), as well as portability, Newer advances on fluorescent sensing assays of pollutant detection can be translated into IOT enabled systems which could form the basis POC devices (Ataei Kachouei et al.). Fluorescence based investigations are popular for many analytes in healthcare like interleukin-8 (Verbarg et al., 2017), malarial biomarker (pLDH) protein (Kenry et al., 2016), anthrax biomarker (Bhattacharyya et al., 2017), acute myocardial infarction (Tabish et al., 2022), and various environmental analytes like picric acid (Fan et al., 2021), dyes (C. Wu et al., 2018), heavy metal (Devi et al., 2019), Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) (Dolai et al., 2017), etc. The fluorescence-based sensing mechanisms typically use fluorophores (Choudhary and Joshi, 2022), metallic organic framework (MOF) (S. Jain et al., 2023), probes, and Quantum dots (QDs), for the detection of analytes in various complex matrices (Vyas et al., 2022). Various studies have described the use of fluorophores like N,N-carbonyl-bridged dipyrrinone oxime was used to detect the organophosphates pesticides, with a detection limit of 4 ppm (Walton et al., 2012). Among these assays use of fluorescent quantum dots is gaining popularity owing to many advantages like providing various mechanisms of electron transfer, luminescence, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photocurrent generation, fluorescence, etc. with broad excitation & narrow emission, long fluorescence time and higher photo-stability (Choudhary et al., 2021; Dolai et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2018). In addition to the above properties carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have additional advantageous properties over quantum dots like biocompatibility, low toxicity, biological tunability, and cost effectiveness which makes them suitable for wide variety of biological applications (Kelarakis, 2015; Liu et al., 2020). The carbon dots also possess quantum sensing features like optical and electronic properties pertaining to the analysis of signals from the interaction of analyte, bio-recognition element and the transducing carbon atoms in comparison to the interaction at the level of bulk materials. This enables the sensing assay with better precision, accuracy, specificity and overall superior significance (Chugh et al., 2023a; Chugh et al., 2023b).

Carbon dots are used as bio-platform for the colorimetric and fluorometric sensing of OPs (paraoxon) by strategy of using acetylcholinesterase (AChE) which is blocked by OP compounds, and the reaction controlled fluorescence quenching of CQDs with a limit of detection around 0.4 ng/mL (Li et al., 2018). In another study, the Carbon dots-based sensor was used with turn on & off fluorescence-based sensing for the detection of pesticides having mechanism based on the CQDs directly linked with highly reactive –NO2 and –NH functional groups of imidacloprid which have a high affinity of binding with OP compound with a detection limit of 0.013 μM, and efficiency of recovery around 92–97% (Mandal et al., 2019). Similarly, the magnesium dioxide nanosheets conjugated with carbon dots have been used to detect OPs having a limit of detection around 0.015 ng/mL, with a mechanism of FRET between MnO2-CQDs & butryl-cholinesterase triggering the decomposition of MnO2 which provide fluorescence signals from CQDs (Yan et al., 2018). As these method for the detection of OP pesticides have advantages like rapid detection, low detection limit etc., but have several disadvantages like less on-site detection, portability, more requirements of complex and high cost instruments, lack of real time monitoring and multi-analyte detection. These disadvantages need to be focused along with improvisation in analytical parameters of sensing like sensitivity, specificity, limit of detection with point of approaches for real-time on-site monitoring.

Various enzymes have the capability to degrade the organophosphates compounds such as PON1 (Paraoxonase), Diisopropylfluorophosphatase, Phosphotriesterases), OPH (Organophosphorus hydrolyase), OPAA (Organophosphorus Acid Anhydrolase) (Vyas et al., 2022). Out of all these, the OPAA enzyme (EC 3.1.8.2) is one of the promising enzymes that shows specific degradation of OP compounds. OPAA is basically proline dipeptidases that easily split/chop down the P–F bond of OP compounds. Various studies report that wild-type OPAA enzyme acts on various OP compounds and nerve agents (mainly G-type nerve agents) (M. Jain et al., 2021). Many modifications have been made in wild-type OPAA to improve the catalytic efficiency, specificity etc. of the enzyme, such as site specific mutations (Zheng et al., 2022), immobilization of various carrier like liposomes (Petrikovics et al., 2000), spore based display (Song et al., 2019) etc. Mutations in wild-type OPAA with Y212F and V342L mutations named as OPAA-FL variant show 10-fold more catalytic activity than wild-type OPAA (Bae et al., 2018).

In the current investigation, Recombinant OPAA-FL enzyme was expressed in E. coli, induced and purified by affinity chromatography. Organophosphorus acid anhydrolase (OPAA)- Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)-based thin film biosensors were developed and characterized for various optical, structural, elemental, and morphological properties. The OPAA enzyme was immobilized on CQD containing thin films developed from chitosan polymer with an aim to achieve specific detection of two organophosphates pesticides EP and MP in different water matrices as per Scheme 1 . The biosensor so developed was evaluated for different analytical parameters like Limit of Detection (LOD), sensitivity, specificity, accuracy and response time. A spiking study was performed in river water and household water for accuracy of estimation using the developed biosensor. The analytical parameters of synthesized biosensor were compared with HPLC-based detection of OP pesticides.

Phthalic acid, Tri ethylene diamine (TED), Chitosan, Ethyl paraoxon (EP), Methyl parathion (MP), and Manganese Chloride (MnCl2) were procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. Luria-Bertani (LB) broth, Bis tris propane, tris base, and Isopropyl-β-D-Thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) were purchased from HiMedia, India. Acetonitrile and methanol were purchased from Thermofisher, India. Nickel-nitrilotriacetic acid (Ni-NTA) columns were procured from GE Healthcare, India. All solvents and chemicals are HPLC grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OPAA-FL variant expression in E. Coli and purification
ce:section.ce:para: LB broth having Kanamycin (10 μg/ml), was used to inoculate E. coli Rosetta (DE3) cells with the transformation of pET-OPAA-FL vector and were allowed to grow at 37 °C overnight. The cells from the primary culture were inoculated in LB broth and culture incubated in a shaker at 37 °C at 220 rpm, till the Optical Density (OD) reached 0.8 to obtain the secondary culture. The culture was induced with 1 mM of both MnCl2 and IPTG and incubated for 20 h at 20 °C, with 180 rpm in an incubator shaker. After induction, the cells were centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm for cell harvesting. The harvested pellet was washed and suspended with Tris buffer (50 mM) (pH 7.4) with 10% glycerol. Lysozyme (400 μg/ml) was added for the lysis of the cells and then the cells were incubated at 30 °C for 20 min, followed by sonication and centrifugation for 15 min at 15000 rpm. The prepared cell lysates were separated as pellet and supernatant to observe the protein amount in soluble form. The induction of recombinant OPAA -FL protein was confirmed on SDS PAGE gel with 12% w/v.

Since OPAA-FL variant had a N-terminal His-tag, affinity chromatography was performed with Ni-nitrilotriacetic acid column at 4 °C. Firstly the equilibration buffer was prepared having a composition of 50 mM imidazole, 500 mM NaCl and 50 mM Tris-Cl with pH 8.0 and the input sample was made with equilibration buffer and loaded on the column followed with centrifugation at 600g, collected the flow through. The column was then washed twice with washing buffers having a composition of 50 mM-Tris-Cl, pH 8.0, NaCl 500 mM with a change in imidazole concentration having 50 mM in washing buffer 1 and 70 mM in washing buffer 2, respectively. After washing, the elution buffer comprised of 500 mM NaCl, Tris-Cl 50 mM with pH 8.0, and change in concentration of imidazole 100 mM and 150 mM was loaded in the column for better elution of sample protein. The purification of protein samples was confirmed by the SDS PAGE gel with 12%. The Bradford assay was used to evaluate the total concentration of protein purified, for the standard calibration curve the different known concentration of BSA (1 mg/mL) were used in which 100 μL of Bradford reagent was mixed and volume was made with doubled distilled water up to 1 mL. The optical density (OD) were taken at 595 nm, the obtained absorbance standard curve of BSA were used to determine the concentration of purified OPAA protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their thin films
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized from 2.9 M TED and 2M phthalic acid and were mixed in 1:1 in 9 ml of double distilled water according to a protocol adapted from Choudhary et al. (2021). After mixing the solution was kept in the microwave oven (Samsung, Malaysia) at 750 Watts for 2 min. The solution was allowed to cool to room temperature after exposure and filtered through a membrane filter of 0.2-μm size, after which the suspension was kept for dialysis with a 14 kDa molecular cutoff membrane in 40 mL of double distilled water. The filtered and dialyzed solution was lyophilized to get powdered CQDs. The CQDs were used to prepare films using chitosan polymer 5% (w/v) solution by mixing into the 40 ml of double distilled water and kept on stirring for 2 h. The synthesized CQDs were added to the chitosan solution 10% w/v of the total volume of the solution. The solutions were allowed to evaporate to solidify the polymer film. The thin films containing CQDs so formed were cut into small circular discs with a paper punch cutter having approx. size (5 mm).

The optical characterization of synthesized CQDs was done through fluorescence measured on a fiber optic spectrometer (FOS) device (Ocean Insight, USA) and absorbance with a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV1900I, Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM CM 20, Philips, India) was used to analyze the morphology and size distribution of the CQDs, in which copper gird of mesh size 400 μm was used, and CQDs were drop casted on it for TEM analysis. The composition and chemical characteristics of CQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Beamline PES-BL-14, India). The lattice and crystalline parameters of CQDs were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), (XRD Rigaku, Japan), smart lab diffraction with Cu X-ray (1.54 Å). The photostability of CQDs was analyzed by a FOS device over a period of 60 days. To evaluate CQDs distribution in CQDs thin films, Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (IX83, Olympus, Japan), was used. The intensity of thin films was evaluated using a reflectance probe attached to a fiber optic spectrometer. The characterization of CQDs thin films was compared to bare CQDs.

Enzyme activity studies for OPAA-FL variant protein were performed with the two substrates EP and MP in the solution phase and in the immobilized films. In the activity assay, 1 μl of cell lysate was mixed with Bis-Tris Propane buffer (50 mM) pH 8.5, 1 mM MnCl2, along with 1 mM EP/MP with a total volume to 250 μl with doubled distilled water. The activity of the OPAA-FL variant was calculated by measuring the increase in the p-nitro-phenol absorbance, which is the end product of substrate degradation at 410 nm. The quantification of protein was done using extinction coefficient of p-nitro-phenol which is 17000 M/cm. Similarly, the immobilized OPAA enzyme activity on CQDs thin films was also measured with the above-mentioned protocol, to determine the enzyme activity of immobilized OPAA on CQDs thin film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensing of OP compounds with OPAA-CQDs thin film based biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The various concentration of both OP compounds ethyl paraoxon and methyl parathion were taken in the range 0–100 μM were exposed to the OPAA-CQDs synthesized thin film biosensor for 5 min and fluorescence of the thin film was recorded through FOS device. The pH of the solution of concentrations was also recoded simultaneously to observe the changes in the pH through the pH meter (Hanna, Italy). The calibration curve was plotted with the OP compound concentration and fluorescence intensity of the OPAA-CQDs thin film. The analytical parameters are calculated from the calibration curve such as resolution, linear range, sensitivity, detection limit etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spiking study in household tap water and river water
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized OPAA-CQDs based thin film biosensor was evaluated against the different concentrations of EP and MP by capturing the fluorescence intensities at 505 nm. The detection range (0–100 μM) of substrates was decided as per the permissible limit for sensing studies. The response of fluorescence emission was captured in the FOS device in triplicates. The predicted/estimated concentrations of OPs in various water resources were obtained using a calibration curve. The river samples used in the biosensing were obtained from Narmada River (Omkareshwar, Khandwa, India) and the Kshipra River (Ujjain, India), and these river samples were collected in the month of November 2022. Household tap water was taken from the water supply in Simrol, Indore, Madhya Pradesh, India. The spiking experiment was used to calculate the analytical parameters like sensitivity, accuracy, reproducibility, resolution, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-based detection of OPs
ce:section.ce:para: HPLC (Prominence-i LC-2030 C (Shimadzu, Japan)) instrument was used in the detection of OP compounds as a comparison against the fiber optic spectrometer-based biosensing study. An analytical method was adapted from Mdeni et al. (2022), for the quantification of EP/MP. Briefly, a C18 (5 μm) (4.6 × 250 mm) HPLC column (Shimadzu, Japan) was used with mobile phase in isocratic mode comprised of 70: 30 of Acetonitrile: MiliQ water. The UV detector was set at 275 nm with a flow rate of 0.6 ml/min for both substrates. A calibration curve was developed for EP and MP and used for the quantification of spiked samples from different water resources (river water and household water).

For the evaluation of specificity of OPAA-CQDs thin film, various OP compounds (Malathion, Coumaphos, Dimethoate, EP &MP) were taken in the same concentration to contact with The OPAA-CQDs thin film specificity. OPAA enzyme stability and overall performance of OPAA-CQD thin film biosensor for detection of OPs was evaluated over a period of 15 days. The OPAA-CQD thin films were exposed to some concentrations of EP and MP and fluorescence response captured in a manner like biosensing experiments. Similarly, the photo-stability of CQDs thin film, fluorescence were recorded of thin film at regular interval for over two months. The OPAA-CQD thin films were stored between 2 and 8 °C in a refrigerator until analysis.

Statistical relevance of experimental data, the correlation Pearson coefficient (r.) and t-test (student paired) were calculated. The mean of the 2 groups (real groups & predicted/estimated groups) are either equal or significantly different was calculated by t-test. The R-value defines the significance of the actual value, and it shows high significance or similarity to the real value if it is more than 0.95.

The OPAA wild-type enzyme gene was encoded initially from Alteromonas sp. JD6.5 (Cheng and Defrank, 2000), using site-directed mutagenesis. Various mutants have been reported with wild-type OPAA for better catalytic efficiency and specificity towards OP compounds (M. Jain et al., 2019). One of the mutants OPAA-FL variant has unique mutations V342L and Y212F which increased its catalytic efficiency up to 10 fold more than wild-type OPAA towards OP compounds like EP, MP, and Soman (M. Jain et al., 2021). The OPAA-FL (Mol wt. approx. 58 kDa) induction and purification were confirmed by the SDS-PAGE gel (12%) in both stages separately. The expression of OPAA-FL was mainly observed in the soluble fraction supernatant (Fig. 1 A, lane 3). After expression of OPAA-FL, purification was performed, and observed in flow-through where most of the protein was absent (Fig. 1B, lane 2), and a purification of more the 90% of pure variant protein of OPAA-FL was achieved (Fig. 1B, lane 5). In the specific activity of various samples of OPAA-FL variant with EP (Fig. 1C), purified OPAA-FL variant protein shows higher specific activity 3.5-fold times more (1.18 U/mg) as compared to the induced cell lysate having 0.5 U/mg. Un-induced cell lysate exhibited least specific activity. In specific activity with MP (Fig. 1D), the purified OPAA-FL variant shows higher specific activity 5 times more (0.53 U/mg), as compared with induced cell lysate having 0.1 U/mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs thin films and co-immobilization of OPAA in thin films
ce:section.ce:para: A simple facile one-step microwave-based method was used for the synthesis of CQDs (Choudhary et al., 2021). Briefly, in a microwave oven, the phthalic acid and TED were kept in an equal ratio (in the absence of oxygen) so that pyrolysis is possible. The aromatic compound with a long chain compound (like phthalic acid) and a passavant (like TED) was used to synthesize the CQD's with long wavelength emission, which has unique optical properties with amine structure to enhance the quantum yield of CQDs (Choudhary et al., 2021). Phthalic acid and TED reorganization along with fast carbonation was confirmed by CQDs synthesis. CQDs with mixed with 5% w/v chitosan to form CQDs containing thin films. OPAA immobilization on the CQD containing film was based on electrostatic attraction as OPAA enzyme has a negative charge and chitosan is positive, it is easily immobilized on the CQDs thin film. Similarly, the specific activity of OPAA immobilized on CQDs thin film was determined with the same protocol and it was found to be similar to the purified enzyme of OPAA-FL in the solution phase for both EP (1.10 U/mg) (Fig. 1C) and for MP (0.51 U/mg) (Fig. 1D), it indicates OPAA-FL immobilized on thin films is have same activity with purified OPAA-FL and activity of OPAA-FL is not affected by the immobilization process on thin films.

The chemical composition of synthesized CQDs was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In analysis of CQDs thin films, three main peaks were observed at 528 eV for O1s, and another at 289 eV for C1s (Fig. 2 A), which is very similar as compared to the bare CQDs solution (Fig. S1) All these peaks indicate the presence of 3 elements mainly nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon along with confirmation of bonds mainly C=O, C–C/C=C, and C–O–H bonds. The intensity of peak of nitrogen at 404eV was found to decrease due immobilization in the chitosan films. The XPS data of synthesized CQDs was like a previous study by Lee et al. (Lee et al., 2018). TEM was used to analyze the morphological properties of synthesized CQDs, and the data images of TEM showed that the distribution of CQDs has monodispersed with size ranges 10±3 nm (Fig. 2B). Optical characterization of synthesized CQD containing films, was done with a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV, 1900i; Shimadzu, Japan) to analyze the absorption spectra of CQDs, and it was observed that CQDs have excitation at λ max = 365 nm, emission peak maximum was observed at 505 nm (green color) (Fig. 2C) characterized using a fiber optic spectrometer (FOS) (Flame, Ocean optics, US), which is similar to the bare CQDs (Fig. S2). The result suggests that immobilization of CQDs in chitosan films does not influence the optical properties maintaining the excitation and emission wavelength which were found with bare CQDs. The analysis of the crystalline morphology of synthesized CQDs X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed, which indicates that CQDs shows a prominent peak at 24.5o, indicating the plane (002) with 0.36 nm value of D spacing. The characteristic peak of carbon occurred at 2Ѳ value between 22 and 28 °2Ѳ (Lin et al., n.d.). The analysis of XRD was found similar to the previous studies with bare CQDs in the solution phase (Fig. 2) (Choudhary et al., 2021). Fig. 2D indicates the XRD analyses of CQDs thin film suggest the amorphous nature of CQDs as it immobilized in the chitosan thin film. CLSM was used to evaluate the distribution of fluorophores in CQDs thin films, even distribution of CQDs was observed with uniform fluorescence throughout the thin film and the fluorescence intensity graph is constant throughout the film (Fig. 2E and F). The characterization of CQDs thin film were found to be similar with bare CQDs in solution phase which indicates that the property of CQDs in thin films is undisturbed and they are present in its original state without any change in their property.

In the presence of organophosphate substrates i.e., EP & MP in any water matrices, OPAA enzyme (linked with CQDs thin film) will degrade the substrate into its product para-nitro phenol and diethyl phosphoric acid and dimethyl phosphoric acid respectively along with the release of two protons in alkaline medium as mentioned in Fig. 3 . The synthesized CQDs thin film is pH-sensitive in nature and shows overall pH sensitivity in the range pH 3 to 10. As the pH increases from pH 3 to pH 10, the CQD thin film shows an increase in its fluorescence (Fig. 4 A and B). This property of pH sensitivity of CQDs thin film is used to detect OP pesticides in conjunction with OPAA. Due to the release of H+/acid in the reaction of OPAA and OP, the pH of the microenvironment of films decreases. Due to the change in pH, pH sensitive CQDs in the thin film provide a change in their fluorescence emission at 505 nm. The calibration curve was developed for different concentrations (0–100 μM) of EP & MP with OPAA-CQDs thin film. The calibration curve was found to be linear with decreasing trend as the pH is decreasing. pH sensitivity of OPAA-CQDs thin film was evaluated and was found to have linear relation with the increasing pH (Fig. 4B).

The fluorescence response CQDs thin film with both organophosphates’ substrates EP& MP having linear regression equation for EP (y = −0.0062x + 0.959) (Fig. 5 A) & MP (y = −0.0078x +1.0) in (Fig. 5B). The sensitivity of sensing reaction was calculated by the ratio of the intensity change with per unit change of concentration of OP substrates. For EP, the sensitivity was found to be 0.0049 units/μM of EP along with R2 = 0.986, and for MP, it was 0.0078/μM with R2 = 0.992. In the case of CQDs thin film without immobilization of OPAA, the calibration curve was found to be a straight line no pH change or any other effect with EP & MP (Fig. 5A and B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spiking study in household tap water and river water
ce:section.ce:para: The household tap water and river samples (Narmada and Kshipra) were used for spiking different concentrations of both EP/MP and then tested using OPAA-CQDs thin films. The thin film fluorescence intensity was measured with the aim to check the suitability and evaluate interferences present in real water resources for biosensing OPs. The values of accuracy were expressed in terms of average percentage recovery which was found to be improved than existing methods. In household tap water, (Fig. 5C) the % recovery was found to be 100.5% for EP and 102.6% for MP (Fig. 5D). The corresponding mean error values were found to be 0.5–2.63%. The values indicate that the components in household drinking water have negligible interference with estimation of spiked EP and MP. Considering the water samples in the household supply may have been purified, filtered and treated, direct water resources like river water (Narmada & Kshipra Rivers) (Fig. 5C & D) was evaluated for spiking studies. In Narmada River water samples, the percentage recovery for EP and MP was found to be 101.7 % and 101 % with a mean error of 1–1.7%, respectively.

Similarly, in the case of Kshipra River water samples, the percentage recovery for EP and MP was calculated as 102.5 % and 101 %, respectively, with a mean error of 1.7–2.5%. In all the spiking studies it is clear that the OPAA-CQD film biosensor has an excellent accuracy (∼100%) of estimation for EP and MP in all three-water resources i.e., household tap water, Narmada and Kshipra River water. This indicates that the OPAA-CQDs thin film biosensor has a great suitability to detect both EP and MP even in real water samples.

The EP & MP detection prediction has calculated r values of 0.98 and 0.99 respectively, with P (t stat ≤ t critical) > 0.05. These statistical data indicate the p-value t stat ≤ t critical, and P value > 0.05, which suggests the null hypothesis in which two groups have equal means which cannot be rejected. These r value and student paired t-test suggests the OPAA-CQDs thin film suitability for the detection of EP and MP compared to control CQD films.

The results of biosensing of OPs using FOS were compared with HPLC based measurement considering it is a sophisticated analytical laboratory technique. The detection of both OPs was done using HPLC at different concentrations (0–100 μm). Using a C18 column and a mobile phase comprised of acetonitrile: deionized water (70:30), with a flow rate of 0.6 ml/min a retention time (RT) of EP, was found at 7.46 min (Fig. S4A) and 11.6 min (Fig. S4B) for MP with 275 nm detection. The calibration curves were plotted for both the OPs compounds with the concentration ranges 0–100 μM. The calibration of both the OP compounds with HPLC are found to be linear with the regression equation for EP (Fig. 6 A) as y = 0.33x + 1.51 and similarly for the MP (Fig. 6B) it is y = 1.808x - 9.77. The sensitivity of EP was found to be 0.33/μM with the linearity in the terms of the regression coefficient (R2) = 0.997, in a similar fashion for the MP it is found to be 1.80/μM with R2 = 0.998. After plotting the calibration curves, the spike, and recovery studies of both OP compounds were done in the three matrices of water (household tap water, Narmada, and Kshipra River samples) with the concentration range of 0–100 μM. In tap water, the average percentage recovery for EP (Fig. 6C), was found to be 101.11% with a mean error of 1.11%., similarly for the MP (Fig. 6C), it was found to be 101.37% ± 1.37%.

In the case of Narmada River samples, the accuracy in terms of the average percentage recovery for the EP (Fig. 6C) is 101.04% with a mean error is 1.04%, similarly, for the MP (Fig. 6D), it was found to be 101.10% ± 1.10%. In a similar fashion, in Kshipra River samples, the average percentage recovery for ethyl parathion (Fig. 6C) is 101.07% ± 1.07%, and for MP (Fig. 6D), it was found to be 100.90 & with a mean error of 0.90%.

The analytical parameters of detection of EP and MP with CQDs-OPAA-based thin film and sophisticated instrument HPLC, like sensitivity, resolution, linearity, the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantification (LOQ) is compared in Table 1 . The linearity in terms of the regression coefficient (R2) for both HPLC and CQDs the linearity is comparable with only a little difference.

In comparison, it was found that as HPLC is a highly sophisticated and standard instrument for analysis, obviously its sensitivity is high, in this study HPLC is more sensitive in both OP compounds as 6-fold more sensitive in EP 0and 23-fold more sensitive in MP compared to CQDs thin film-based detection using a fiber optic spectrometer set up. In the case of resolution, the CQDs thin film can resolute 1.8-fold more than HPLC based detection in EP while in methyl parathion, HPLC is able to resolve three-fold more than CQDs thin film-based detection.

The CQDs based detection of EP has linearity of 0.98 and 0.99 for MP, both of linearity of CQDs thin film which are nearby similar to linearity of HPLC based detection which have linearity of 0.99 for both EP and MP. The CQDs thin film based detection for the detection of EP has two-time lower limit of detection (LOD), as compared to the HPLC based detection of EP, while HPLC has three times lower limit of detection (LOD) of MP than CQDs thin film-based detection. Similarly, with respect to the limit of quantification (LOQ), the CQDs thin film shows twice fold lower limit of quantification (LOQ) than HPLC in detection of EP, while in MP the HPLC shows thrice lower limit of quantification (LOQ) than CQDs thin film-based detection. The CQDs thin film-based detection of EP have two times more resolution than HPLC based detection, while in detection of MP the HPLC based detection have thrice fold more resolution than CQDs thin film-based detection. The sensitivity of HPLC is ultimately higher as it is highly sophisticated instrument in both EP (53 more-fold) & MP (230 more-fold) detection then CQDs thin film-based detection. The accuracy in the terms of percentage recovery of both substrates’ EP and MP is almost comparable in both CQDs thin film based and HPLC based detection methods. The comparison of all analytical parameters between HPLC and CQDs based thin film detection are found to be nearly similar in and it shows that OPAA-CQD based thin film has potential as a biosensor for the detection of the organophosphate pesticides with good sensitivity and accuracy along with lower LOD and LOQ with better resolution in a lesser response of time. An economic analysis POC fiber optic device is five times less expensive than a HPLC. Considering the running cost in point of care devices consisting of thin film biosensors and POC devices will be inexpensive to the running cost of tests done on sophisticated instruments like HPLC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of OPAA-CQDs thin film biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the sensing strategy of OPAA_CQDs thin film-based detection was evaluated by exposing wide variety of OP compounds in working concentrations (Fig. 7 A). The sensitivity of EP and MP response was found to be 65 and 71%, respectively. The sensitivity for the other OP compound (Dimethoate, Coumaphos and Malathion) was found to be less than 10%. It shows that OPAA-CQDs thin film is highly specific for EP and MP in comparison to other tested OP compounds making this strategy of OPAA-CQD film biosensing a novel biosensor. The photo-stability of the CQD thin films, the fluorescence response of the CQDs thin film was checked at regular intervals by exciting the CQDs at 365 nm and recording the emission, it was found that CQDs thin films shows excellent photo-stability for over 60 days (Fig. 7B) with only a 1–2% drop in fluorescence emission with minimum photo-bleaching. To evaluate the stability of immobilized OPAA enzyme on CQDs thin film and biosensing stability biosensing study was evaluated over 2 weeks with detection of EP and MP (Fig. 7C and S5). The results show that biosensing assay is stable up to 10 days. OPAA enzyme shows activity till 10 days with over 80% activity retention and after which on 12th day the activity retention was 55% and on 15th day activity was retention was 45% Thus the stability analysis shows that OPAA enzyme is stable up to 11th days with 1–2% drop in its activity.

The comparison of the present study in the Table 2 , indicates the present study have the novelty of fluorescence-based detection of specifically two OP compound at a single platform with a point of care approach, real-time monitoring, and having a distinctly low LOD and detection range is found to be similar and better with the existing investigation which mainly have the electrochemical mechanism with complex detection and colorimetric/fluorescence-based detection with high LOD.

This study demonstrates the application of Organophosphorus acid anhydrolase (OPAA) enzyme-Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) based thin film-based biosensors for the specific detection of two organophosphates pesticides: Ethyl Paraoxon (EP) and Methyl parathion (MP). The degradation of these two OP compounds by the OPAA enzyme into its end product p-nitro phenol and phosphoric acid with the production of 2 protons (H+), cause decrease in the pH of the micro-environment of films. The change in pH due to spiking of OPs or actual presence of OPs in water matrices will lead to change in fluorescence of CQDs in the thin films. The analytical parameter of sensing studies of OPAA-CQDs thin film were compared with a sensitive HPLC instruments and the result suggest that sensing parameters are comparable in both (HPLC & OPAA-CQDs based thin film) for EP & MP detection. The synthesized OPAA-CQDs thin film-based biosensors has a potential to show the point-of use approach for the detection of OPs with good sensitivity (0.0062–0.0078/μM), better accuracy between 100% and 102% with a minimal error of 2% and having a lower limit of detection 0.18 & 0.69 ppm. The results show that the OPAA-CQDs thin film-based biosensor can be used for the specific detection of EP and MP with real-time monitoring in a low response time of 5 min in all three real samples of water matrices.
